note_data.title: Double entry book-keeping – Part A
keyPoints: The process of accounting starts once the book-keeper finishes recording the financial transactions
Accounting deals with measuring, identifying, and communicating financial information recorded by the book-keeper which facilitates the preparation of financial statements
Bookkeeping involves recording financial transactions from source documents of a business whereas accounting deals with maintaining financial statements from the bookkeeping records
Assets are the resources owned and controlled by a company - an increase in an asset is a debit and a decrease is a credit
Every asset can be categorised between two: Tangible and Intangible
Current Assets are assets which have a monetary value or can be converted into cash within 12 months
Non-Current Assets (also known as Fixed Assets) are assets acquired to earn revenue for the company and used for more than 12 months without being intended for resale
Capital is the number of resources and funds provided by the owner injected into the business - an increase in capital is a credit while a decrease or drawings is a debit
Liability is the money owed to another firm or an entity

definitions.definition: The resources owned and controlled by a company
The number of resources and funds provided by the owner injected into the business
The money owed to another firm or an entity
The intangible asset that represents the reputation, customer loyalty, brand value, and other non-physical attributes of a business
An asset which has a monetary value or can be converted into cash within 12 months

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Set out the ledger account with proper headings, debit/credit sides, and balances

modelAnswer.question: Explain the principle of double entry book-keeping.
modelAnswer.answer: Double entry book-keeping requires every transaction to be recorded twice. Each transaction is recorded once as a debit entry in one account and once as a credit entry in another account. This ensures that the accounting equation (Assets = Capital + Liabilities) always remains balanced.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Once as debit, once as credit

commonMistakes: Mixing up debit and credit entries for assets and capital
Not distinguishing between tangible and intangible assets
Confusing current assets with non-current assets based on the 12-month rule
examinerTips: Remember the basic accounting equation: Assets = Capital + Liabilities
Use the formulas provided: Capital = Assets - Liabilities and Liabilities = Assets - Capital
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-account format showing debit side (left) and credit side (right) with proper headings including account name, date, details, and amount columns
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q2 (6 marks) — they asked students to prepare journal entries for various business transactions and explain the dual aspect concept
O/N 2022 P1 Q1 (8 marks) — candidates had to identify which accounts would be debited and credited for purchases, sales, and expenses transactions
M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — students were required to explain the accounting equation and show how it remains balanced after recording transactions
O/N 2021 P1 Q4 (7 marks) — question focused on recording transactions in T-accounts and explaining the rules of debit and credit

workedExample.question: On 1 May 2023, Sarah's business had the following transactions: (a) Purchased goods for cash $500 (b) Sold goods on credit to J. Smith $800 (c) Paid rent $200 by cheque. Prepare journal entries for these transactions and explain the dual aspect concept. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the accounts affected for each transaction and classify them (asset, liability, capital, income, expense)
Step 2: Apply debit/credit rules - Assets and Expenses increase on debit side, Liabilities, Capital and Income increase on credit side
Step 3: Transaction (a): Purchases (expense) increases so debit Purchases $500, Cash (asset) decreases so credit Cash $500
Step 4: Transaction (b): Accounts Receivable (asset) increases so debit Accounts Receivable $800, Sales (income) increases so credit Sales $800
Step 5: Transaction (c): Rent (expense) increases so debit Rent $200, Bank (asset) decreases so credit Bank $200
Step 6: Explain dual aspect: every transaction affects two or more accounts, total debits always equal total credits
workedExample.answer:
Journal Entries:
(a) Dr Purchases $500
    Cr Cash $500
(b) Dr Accounts Receivable $800
    Cr Sales $800
(c) Dr Rent $200
    Cr Bank $200

Dual aspect concept: Every business transaction has two aspects and affects two or more accounts. The total value of debits must always equal the total value of credits, maintaining the accounting equation Assets = Liabilities + Capital.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for each correct journal entry (1 mark for correct accounts, 1 mark for correct debit/credit), 2 marks for explaining dual aspect concept with reference to equal debits and credits

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused which accounts to debit and credit when recording expenses' — always remember expenses and assets increase on the debit side, while income and liabilities increase on the credit side

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to include dollar signs and failed to align figures properly in journal entries' — always format journal entries clearly with proper indentation for credit entries and include currency symbols

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates often provided incomplete explanations of the dual aspect concept' — always mention that every transaction affects at least two accounts AND that total debits equal total credits when explaining dual aspect
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the standard T-account format showing the account name at the top, with a vertical line dividing debit (left) and credit (right) sides. They should also be able to diagram the accounting equation: Assets = Liabilities + Capital, and show how this equation remains balanced after transactions. Additionally, students should memorize and draw the debit/credit rules chart showing which account types increase on debit side (Assets, Expenses) versus credit side (Liabilities, Capital, Income).
note_data.quickRecap: Accounting begins after bookkeeping is complete and involves measuring, identifying, and communicating financial information to prepare financial statements. The fundamental elements are Assets (resources owned by the company), Capital (owner's investment), and Liabilities (money owed to others). These are connected through the basic accounting equation: Assets = Capital + Liabilities.
